key_insight: A temporal, multimodal MRI model can flag prostate cancer progression during active surveillance by learning changes across serial scans without lesion segmentation.
clinical_relevance: This approach could help reduce unnecessary repeat biopsies while still identifying patients who are more likely to have true pathological progression.
method: Encode each longitudinal MRI with a pretrained 3D foundation model
Use a temporal attention gate to emphasize progression-related changes across visits
Fuse gated imaging features with clinical variables to predict progression risk
evidence_signal: In a longitudinal active surveillance cohort, the model outperformed competing baselines and performed comparably to radiologist assessment while maintaining high negative predictive value and higher positive predictive value.
watch_item: Performance may depend on access to serial MRI, consistent scan quality, and external validation before use in routine surveillance decisions.
paper_title: TRACE-PCa: Predicting Prostate Cancer Progression from Longitudinal MRI During Active Surveillance
